Most Total Successors Priority Rule (MTS:
Step 1: Parse the project network and extract all activities, their durations, resource requirements, precedence relationships, and available resource capacities.
Step 2: Build the Activity-on-Node (AON) network graph where each node represents an activity and directed edges represent finish-to-start precedence constraints.
Step 3: Add a dummy source activity (activity 0) with zero duration and zero resource requirements that precedes all activities with no predecessors, and a dummy sink activity (activity n+1) with zero duration and zero resource requirements that succeeds all activities with no successors.
Step 4: Compute the set of all successors (direct and indirect) for each activity by performing a reverse topological traversal of the network. For each activity i, the total successors set includes all activities that can only start after activity i is completed, either directly or transitively.
Step 5: Calculate the MTS priority value for each activity by counting the total number of successors (both direct and indirect) it has in the network. An activity with more total successors receives a higher priority value.
Step 6: Initialize the schedule by setting the current time t = 0, marking the dummy source activity as completed, and placing all activities that have no predecessors (or whose only predecessor is the dummy source) into the eligible activity list.
Step 7: Identify the Decision Set (DS) at the current time t, which consists of all activities whose predecessors have all been completed and that have not yet been scheduled.
Step 8: From the Decision Set, identify the Schedulable Set (SS) by filtering out activities whose resource requirements exceed the currently available resource capacities at time t.
Step 9: Sort the activities in the Schedulable Set in descending order of their MTS priority values (i.e., the activity with the most total successors is ranked first). In case of ties in MTS values, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as Latest Finish Time (LFT) or random selection.
Step 10: Select the highest-priority activity from the sorted Schedulable Set and schedule it to start at the current time t. Assign the required resources to this activity for its duration.
Step 11: Update the available resource capacities by subtracting the resource requirements of the newly scheduled activity for each time period it will be in progress (from t to t + duration - 1).
Step 12: Remove the scheduled activity from the Decision Set and update the list of completed activities and in-progress activities accordingly.
Step 13: Repeat Steps 9 through 12, continuing to select and schedule the next highest-priority feasible activity from the updated Schedulable Set at the current time t, until no more activities can be scheduled at time t (either the Schedulable Set is empty or remaining activities exceed available resources).
Step 14: If no activities can be scheduled at the current time t, advance the time to the next event point. The next event point is the earliest completion time among all currently in-progress activities.
Step 15: At the new time t, release the resources used by all activities that have just completed, and add their direct successors (whose all predecessors are now completed) to the Decision Set.
Step 16: Repeat Steps 7 through 15 until all activities, including the dummy sink, have been scheduled and completed.
Step 17: Calculate the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy sink activity (or the maximum finish time among all real activities).
Step 18: Record the final schedule, including the start time, finish time, and resource usage for each activity, and report the total project makespan as the solution produced by the MTS heuristic.
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Parse the problem input by reading all project data, including the number of jobs/activities, the number of resource types, the resource capacities for each resource type, the processing duration for each activity, the resource requirements per unit time for each activity and each resource type, and the precedence constraints (finish-to-start dependencies) between activities.
Step 2: Build the precedence network by constructing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each node represents an activity and each directed edge represents a precedence constraint from a predecessor activity to a successor activity.
Step 3: Compute the Earliest Start Time (EST) for each activity using a forward pass through the precedence network. Start with the project start node (activity 0) having EST = 0, then for each activity in topological order, set EST[j] = max(EST[i] + duration[i]) for all predecessors i of activity j.
Step 4: Compute the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity using a backward pass through the precedence network. Start with the project end node having LFT = the critical path length, then for each activity in reverse topological order, set LFT[j] = min(LFT[k] - duration[k]) for all successors k of activity j.
Step 5: Calculate the Greatest Resource Demand (GRD) priority value for each activity. The GRD priority is computed as the sum over all resource types of (resource requirement of the activity for that resource type divided by the total available capacity of that resource type), i.e., GRD[j] = sum_k(r[j][k] / R[k]), where r[j][k] is the requirement of activity j for resource k and R[k] is the capacity of resource k.
Step 6: Initialize the scheduling environment by setting the current time t = 0, marking all activities as unscheduled, setting the set of completed activities to empty, setting the set of activities in progress to empty, and initializing the remaining resource availability at each time point to the full resource capacities.
Step 7: Identify the set of eligible activities (also called the decision set or schedulable set) at the current time t. An activity j is eligible if all its predecessors have been completed (i.e., their finish times are <= t) and the activity has not yet been scheduled.
Step 8: From the eligible set, identify the set of feasible activities by checking resource constraints. An activity j is feasible at time t if, for every resource type k, the current remaining resource availability at time t is greater than or equal to r[j][k] (the resource requirement of activity j for resource k).
Step 9: If the feasible set is non-empty, rank all feasible activities in descending order of their GRD priority value computed in Step 5. Activities with higher GRD values are given higher scheduling priority. Break ties using a secondary criterion such as Latest Finish Time (LFT) in ascending order (i.e., activities with smaller LFT are scheduled first among ties).
Step 10: Select the highest-priority feasible activity from the ranked list (the activity with the greatest GRD value) and schedule it to start at the current time t. Record its start time as ST[j] = t and its finish time as FT[j] = t + duration[j].
Step 11: Update the resource availability profile by reducing the available units of each resource type k by r[j][k] for each time unit in the interval [ST[j], FT[j]) to reflect the resource consumption of the newly scheduled activity.
Step 12: Add the scheduled activity to the set of activities in progress and remove it from the eligible set. Repeat Steps 9 through 11 (continue scheduling feasible activities at the current time t) until no more feasible activities can be scheduled at time t, either because the eligible set is empty or because no eligible activity can be accommodated within the current resource availability.
Step 13: Advance the current time t to the next relevant event point. The next event time is the minimum finish time among all activities currently in progress, i.e., t = min(FT[j]) for all j in the in-progress set. This corresponds to the earliest time at which a resource will be freed and new activities may become eligible or feasible.
Step 14: Update the set of completed activities by moving all in-progress activities whose finish time equals the new current time t into the completed set, and free their resource allocations accordingly.
Step 15: Repeat Steps 7 through 14 iteratively until all activities have been scheduled and completed (i.e., the completed set contains all activities including the project end node).
Step 16: Extract the final schedule by recording the start time and finish time of every activity. Compute the project makespan as the finish time of the last activity (the project end node), which represents the total duration of the project under the GRD priority rule.
Step 17: Validate the generated schedule by verifying two conditions: (a) Precedence feasibility — for every precedence constraint (i, j), confirm that FT[i] <= ST[j]; and (b) Resource feasibility — for every time unit t and every resource type k, confirm that the total resource consumption at time t does not exceed the resource capacity R[k].
Step 18: Optionally, compare the makespan obtained by the GRD heuristic with other priority rules (such as Minimum Latest Finish Time, Most Total Successors, or Minimum Slack) or with the optimal solution if available, to evaluate the quality of the GRD schedule and determine if further improvement through metaheuristics or exact methods is warranted.
Most Work Remaining Priority Rule (MWKR):
Step 1: Parse the RCPSP instance data, including the number of jobs/activities, the number of resource types, the resource capacities for each resource type, the duration of each activity, the resource requirements of each activity for each resource type, and the precedence constraints (finish-to-start dependencies) between activities.
Step 2: Build a precedence graph (directed acyclic graph) where each node represents an activity and each directed edge represents a precedence constraint from a predecessor activity to a successor activity.
Step 3: Add a dummy source activity (activity 0) with zero duration and zero resource requirements that precedes all activities with no predecessors, and a dummy sink activity (activity n+1) with zero duration and zero resource requirements that succeeds all activities with no successors.
Step 4: Compute the 'Most Work Remaining' (MWKR) priority value for each activity. This is defined as the sum of durations of all activities that lie on any path from the current activity to the sink activity (including the activity itself), representing the total remaining work in the longest chain or the sum of all successor durations including itself.
Step 5: Specifically, calculate the MWKR value for each activity j as: MWKR(j) = duration(j) + sum of durations of all activities that are successors of j (directly or transitively), which can be computed via a reverse topological traversal of the precedence graph.
Step 6: Perform a topological sort of the precedence graph to determine a valid processing order and to facilitate the computation of successor sets and MWKR values.
Step 7: Initialize the schedule by setting the current time t = 0, marking all resource availabilities at their full capacities, setting all activities as unscheduled, and identifying the initial set of eligible activities (those whose all predecessors have been completed).
Step 8: At each scheduling decision point (current time t), identify the set of activities that are currently being processed (in-progress activities) and update the remaining resource availability by subtracting the resources consumed by in-progress activities.
Step 9: Determine the set of schedulable (eligible) activities at time t: these are activities whose all predecessor activities have been completed (finish time <= t) and which have not yet been scheduled.
Step 10: From the set of eligible activities, identify the set of feasible activities: those that can be started at time t without violating any resource capacity constraint, i.e., their resource requirements do not exceed the current available resource capacities for every resource type.
Step 11: Among the feasible activities, rank them in decreasing order of their MWKR priority values (the activity with the highest MWKR value gets the highest priority and is scheduled first).
Step 12: Schedule the highest-priority feasible activity by assigning its start time as t and its finish time as t + duration(activity). Update the resource availability by subtracting the resource requirements of this newly scheduled activity from the available resources.
Step 13: Continue scheduling feasible activities in decreasing order of MWKR values at the current time t, updating resource availability after each assignment, until no more feasible activities can be started at time t (either no eligible activities remain or remaining eligible activities violate resource constraints).
Step 14: If no feasible activity can be scheduled at the current time t (due to resource conflicts), advance the current time t to the next event time, which is the earliest finish time among all currently in-progress activities.
Step 15: When advancing time, update the set of completed activities (those whose finish time <= new t), release the resources consumed by completed activities back to the available resource pool, and update the set of eligible activities by checking if any new activities have all their predecessors completed.
Step 16: Repeat Steps 8 through 15 iteratively until all activities (excluding dummy source and sink) have been scheduled and completed.
Step 17: Once the dummy sink activity is reached and all activities are scheduled, compute the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy sink activity, which equals the maximum finish time among all real activities.
Step 18: Output the final schedule, including the start time and finish time of each activity, and the total project makespan as the objective value of the MWKR heuristic solution.
Step 19: Optionally, validate the solution by checking that all precedence constraints are satisfied (each activity starts no earlier than the finish time of all its predecessors) and that resource constraints are not violated at any time point (the sum of resource requirements of all activities in progress at any time t does not exceed the resource capacity for each resource type).
Step 20: Optionally, compare the MWKR solution quality (makespan) against other priority rules or exact methods to evaluate its effectiveness, and consider using MWKR as an initial solution for metaheuristic approaches such as genetic algorithms, tabu search, or simulated annealing to further improve the schedule.
Least Work Remaining Priority Rule (LWKR:
Step 1: Parse and Initialize Problem Data - Read the RCPSP input data including the set of activities/jobs (including dummy start and end activities), their durations, resource requirements for each resource type, resource capacities, and precedence relationships (finish-to-start dependencies).
Step 2: Build the Precedence Network - Construct a directed acyclic graph (DAG) representing the precedence constraints, where nodes represent activities and directed edges represent finish-to-start dependencies between activities.
Step 3: Compute Total Work Remaining for Each Activity - For each activity, calculate its 'work remaining' value. In LWKR, the work remaining for an activity is typically defined as the sum of durations of all successor activities (including itself) that still need to be completed, representing the remaining workload in its chain.
Step 4: Initialize the Schedule - Set the current time t = 0, mark the dummy start activity as completed, and initialize the set of completed activities, the set of in-progress activities, and the set of scheduled activities.
Step 5: Identify the Eligible Activity Set - At each scheduling decision point (time t), determine the set of activities whose all predecessors have been completed. These activities are candidates for scheduling and form the 'eligible set'.
Step 6: Identify the Feasible Activity Set - From the eligible set, filter out activities that cannot be started due to insufficient available resources. An activity is feasible if, for every resource type k, the sum of resource requirements of all currently in-progress activities plus the resource requirement of the candidate activity does not exceed the resource capacity.
Step 7: Apply the LWKR Priority Rule - For each activity in the feasible set, compute its LWKR priority value. The LWKR priority value is the total remaining work (sum of durations of the activity itself and all its remaining successors in the network). Rank the feasible activities in ascending order of their LWKR values, so that the activity with the least work remaining gets the highest priority.
Step 8: Break Ties - If two or more activities have the same LWKR value, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as selecting the activity with the smallest index, shortest duration, or using another heuristic like Most Total Successors (MTS) or random selection.
Step 9: Schedule the Highest Priority Feasible Activity - Select the activity with the lowest LWKR value (least work remaining) from the feasible set and assign it a start time of t. Update the resource availability by subtracting the resource requirements of the newly scheduled activity from the available resource pool.
Step 10: Update the In-Progress and Scheduled Sets - Add the newly scheduled activity to the in-progress set along with its start time and computed finish time (start time + duration). Record the activity's schedule entry.
Step 11: Repeat Scheduling at Current Time - After scheduling an activity, re-evaluate the feasible set at the current time t (since resources have changed). Continue scheduling feasible activities in LWKR priority order until no more feasible activities can be started at time t.
Step 12: Advance Time to the Next Event - When no more activities can be scheduled at the current time t (either due to resource constraints or an empty eligible set), advance the current time t to the earliest finish time among all in-progress activities.
Step 13: Update Completed Activities - At the new time t, identify all in-progress activities whose finish time equals t. Move these activities from the in-progress set to the completed set and release their consumed resources back to the available resource pool.
Step 14: Recalculate Work Remaining Values (if dynamic) - If using a dynamic version of LWKR, recalculate the work remaining values for all remaining unscheduled activities based on the updated state of the project (e.g., accounting for activities already completed or in progress).
Step 15: Check Termination Condition - Check if all activities (including the dummy end activity) have been scheduled and completed. If yes, the scheduling process is complete. If not, return to Step 5 and repeat the process.
Step 16: Extract the Final Schedule - Once all activities are scheduled, extract the final schedule consisting of the start time and finish time for each activity. Compute the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy end activity.
Step 17: Validate the Schedule - Verify the generated schedule for feasibility by checking that all precedence constraints are satisfied (no activity starts before all its predecessors finish) and all resource constraints are respected at every time point (resource usage never exceeds capacity).
Step 18: Output the Results - Report the final schedule (start and finish times for each activity), the project makespan, and optionally the resource utilization profile over time. If needed, compare the LWKR solution quality against other priority rules or bounds.
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Define the Problem Input - Collect all necessary project data including the set of activities/jobs (including dummy start and end activities), the duration (processing time) of each activity, resource requirements for each activity per resource type, total available capacity for each resource type, and precedence constraints (finish-to-start dependencies) between activities.
Step 2: Build the Precedence Network - Construct a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent activities and directed edges represent precedence relationships. Ensure the network has a single source (dummy start activity with duration 0) and a single sink (dummy end activity with duration 0) to represent the project start and finish.
Step 3: Calculate Earliest Start Times (ES) - Perform a forward pass through the precedence network to compute the earliest start time for each activity. For each activity, ES = maximum of the earliest finish times of all its immediate predecessors, where Earliest Finish Time (EF) = ES + duration.
Step 4: Calculate Latest Start Times (LS) - Perform a backward pass through the precedence network to compute the latest start time for each activity without delaying the project. For each activity, LS = LF - duration, where Latest Finish Time (LF) = minimum of the latest start times of all its immediate successors.
Step 5: Initialize the Scheduling Process - Set the current time t = 0, initialize the schedule as empty, create an empty set for 'Activities in Progress', and create the initial 'Eligible Activity List' containing all activities with no predecessors (typically the dummy start activity).
Step 6: Identify Eligible Activities at Current Time t - At each scheduling period t, determine which activities are eligible to be scheduled. An activity is eligible if: (a) all its predecessor activities have been completed, and (b) it has not yet been scheduled or started.
Step 7: Apply the SPT Priority Rule to Rank Eligible Activities - Sort all currently eligible activities in ascending order of their processing time (duration). Activities with shorter durations receive higher priority and are placed at the top of the ranked list. In case of ties in processing time, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as lowest activity index, minimum latest start time, or minimum resource requirement.
Step 8: Check Resource Availability - For each activity in the SPT-ranked eligible list (from highest to lowest priority), check whether sufficient resources are available at the current time t to execute the activity. Available resources = Total resource capacity - Resources currently consumed by all activities in progress at time t.
Step 9: Schedule Feasible Activities - Iterate through the SPT-ranked eligible list and schedule each activity that satisfies the resource constraints. Assign the activity a start time equal to the current time t and a finish time equal to t + duration. Add the activity to the 'Activities in Progress' set and deduct its resource requirements from the available resource pool.
Step 10: Update the Activities in Progress Set - Remove any activities from the 'Activities in Progress' set whose finish time is less than or equal to the current time t. Release the resources consumed by these completed activities back to the available resource pool.
Step 11: Advance Time to the Next Event - If no eligible activity can be scheduled due to resource constraints, advance the current time t to the next event point. The next event point is the earliest finish time among all activities currently in progress, as completing those activities will free up resources and potentially enable new activities to start.
Step 12: Update the Eligible Activity List - After advancing time and completing activities, update the eligible activity list by adding any new activities whose all predecessors have now been completed and which have not yet been scheduled.
Step 13: Repeat the Scheduling Loop - Return to Step 6 and repeat the process of identifying eligible activities, applying the SPT rule, checking resource availability, scheduling feasible activities, and advancing time until all activities have been scheduled and completed.
Step 14: Detect and Handle the Dummy End Activity - Once all real project activities have been completed, schedule the dummy end activity. Its start time equals the maximum finish time of all its immediate predecessors, and its finish time equals its start time (since its duration is 0). This finish time represents the total project makespan.
Step 15: Record the Final Schedule - Compile the complete schedule by recording the start time and finish time for each activity. Verify that all precedence constraints are satisfied (each activity starts after all its predecessors finish) and all resource constraints are satisfied at every time period.
Step 16: Calculate the Project Makespan - Determine the total project duration (makespan) as the finish time of the dummy end activity, which equals the maximum finish time among all activities in the project.
Step 17: Validate the Solution - Perform a final validation check to confirm: (a) all activities are scheduled exactly once, (b) no precedence constraint is violated, (c) resource usage does not exceed capacity at any time period t, and (d) the computed makespan is consistent with the schedule.
Step 18: Evaluate and Compare the SPT Solution - Since SPT is a heuristic priority rule and does not guarantee an optimal solution for RCPSP, compare the resulting makespan with other priority rules (e.g., LPT, LST, MTS) or with lower bounds (e.g., critical path length, resource-based lower bounds) to assess solution quality.
Step 19: Optionally Apply Local Search or Improvement - If a better solution is desired, use the SPT schedule as an initial solution and apply local search techniques such as activity swapping, forward-backward scheduling passes, or metaheuristic methods (e.g., genetic algorithms, simulated annealing) to improve the makespan.
Step 20: Output the Results - Present the final schedule in a structured format showing each activity's ID, processing time, resource requirements, assigned start time, and finish time, along with the total project makespan and a resource utilization profile over time.
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Step 1: Parse and Initialize Problem Data - Read the RCPSP input data including the set of activities/jobs (including dummy start and end activities), their processing times/durations, resource requirements for each resource type, total available capacity for each resource type, and precedence constraints (finish-to-start dependencies) between activities.
Step 2: Build the Precedence Network - Construct a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent activities and directed edges represent precedence relationships. Each edge from activity i to activity j means activity i must be completed before activity j can start.
Step 3: Compute Earliest Start Times (EST) - Perform a forward pass through the precedence network to calculate the earliest start time for each activity. For the dummy start activity, EST = 0. For all other activities, EST(j) = max(EST(i) + Duration(i)) for all predecessors i of activity j.
Step 4: Compute Latest Finish Times (LFT) - Perform a backward pass through the precedence network to calculate the latest finish time for each activity. For the dummy end activity, LFT = project deadline or the critical path length. For all other activities, LFT(i) = min(LFT(j) - Duration(j)) for all successors j of activity i.
Step 5: Identify the Critical Path - Calculate the total float for each activity as TF(i) = LFT(i) - EST(i) - Duration(i). Activities with TF = 0 lie on the critical path. This information helps understand scheduling priorities.
Step 6: Initialize the Scheduling Environment - Set the current time t = 0, initialize the set of scheduled activities as empty, initialize the set of completed activities as empty, initialize the set of in-progress activities as empty, and set the remaining resource availability for each resource type equal to its total capacity.
Step 7: Determine the Eligible Activity Set at Current Time t - Identify all activities that are eligible for scheduling by checking two conditions: (1) all predecessor activities have been completed (i.e., their finish times are <= t), and (2) the activity has not yet been scheduled or started. This set is called the decision set or eligible set.
Step 8: Apply the LPT Priority Rule to Rank Eligible Activities - Sort all activities in the eligible set in descending order of their processing times (durations). The activity with the longest processing time receives the highest priority. In case of ties in processing time, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as: lowest activity index, smallest latest finish time (LFT), or smallest total float.
Step 9: Check Resource Feasibility and Schedule Activities - Iterate through the sorted eligible activities in LPT order. For each activity, check whether scheduling it at time t is resource-feasible by verifying that the resource requirements of the activity do not exceed the currently available resource capacities for all resource types simultaneously. If feasible, schedule the activity: set its start time = t, set its finish time = t + Duration, deduct its resource requirements from the available resource capacities, and move it from the eligible set to the in-progress set.
Step 10: Handle Resource Infeasibility - If an eligible activity cannot be scheduled at time t due to resource constraints, skip it and continue checking the next activity in the LPT-sorted list. Activities that cannot be scheduled remain in the eligible set for consideration at the next decision point.
Step 11: Advance Time to the Next Decision Point - After processing all eligible activities at time t, determine the next event time. The next decision point is the earliest finish time among all currently in-progress activities, i.e., t_next = min(Finish Time of all in-progress activities). Advance the current time to t_next.
Step 12: Update Completed Activities and Release Resources - At the new current time t, identify all in-progress activities whose finish time equals t. Move these activities to the completed set and release their resource requirements back to the available resource capacities.
Step 13: Repeat the Scheduling Loop - Return to Step 7 and repeat the process of identifying eligible activities, applying the LPT rule, checking resource feasibility, scheduling feasible activities, and advancing time until all activities (including the dummy end activity) have been scheduled and completed.
Step 14: Compute the Project Makespan - Once all activities are scheduled, the project makespan is the finish time of the dummy end activity, which equals the maximum finish time among all real activities. Record the complete schedule including start and finish times for each activity.
Step 15: Validate the Schedule - Verify the final schedule for correctness by checking: (1) all precedence constraints are satisfied (no activity starts before all its predecessors finish), (2) resource constraints are not violated at any time point (total resource usage at any time t does not exceed available capacity for any resource type), and (3) all activities are included in the schedule.
Step 16: Output the Results - Present the final schedule as a table or list showing each activity with its assigned start time, finish time, and duration. Report the total project makespan. Optionally, generate a Gantt chart or resource utilization profile to visualize the schedule and identify any resource peaks or idle periods.
Greatest Rank Positional Weight Priority:
Step 1: Parse the project network to identify all activities, their durations, resource requirements, precedence relationships, and the available resource capacities for each resource type.
Step 2: Construct the Activity-on-Node (AoN) network by representing each activity as a node and each precedence relationship as a directed arc from predecessor to successor activity.
Step 3: Add a dummy start activity (activity 0) and a dummy end activity (activity n+1) to the network, with zero duration and zero resource requirements, to represent the project start and finish.
Step 4: Compute the Late Finish Time (LF) for each activity by performing a backward pass through the network. Start from the dummy end activity and assign it the maximum possible project duration (or critical path length). For each activity, LF = minimum of (LS of all immediate successors).
Step 5: Compute the Late Start Time (LS) for each activity using the formula: LS(i) = LF(i) - d(i), where d(i) is the duration of activity i.
Step 6: Compute the Early Start Time (ES) for each activity by performing a forward pass through the network. ES of the dummy start activity is 0. For each activity, ES(i) = maximum of (EF of all immediate predecessors).
Step 7: Compute the Early Finish Time (EF) for each activity using the formula: EF(i) = ES(i) + d(i).
Step 8: Compute the Total Float (TF) for each activity using the formula: TF(i) = LS(i) - ES(i) = LF(i) - EF(i). Activities with TF = 0 are on the critical path.
Step 9: Compute the Positional Weight (PW) for each activity. The Positional Weight of activity i is defined as the sum of the durations of activity i and all its successors (direct and indirect) in the network: PW(i) = d(i) + sum of d(j) for all j that are successors of i.
Step 10: Compute the Greatest Rank Positional Weight (GRPW) for each activity by ranking all activities based on their Positional Weight values in descending order. Assign rank 1 to the activity with the highest Positional Weight, rank 2 to the next highest, and so on. The GRPW priority value of each activity is its assigned rank (lower rank number = higher priority).
Step 11: In case of ties in Positional Weight values among activities, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as selecting the activity with the greater total resource requirement, the longest duration, or the smallest activity index, depending on the variant of GRPW being used.
Step 12: Initialize the schedule by setting the current time t = 0, marking all activities as unscheduled, and setting the resource availability for each resource type k to its maximum capacity R(k) at all time periods.
Step 13: At each scheduling step, identify the set of eligible activities (also called the decision set or schedulable set). An activity is eligible if: (a) all its predecessor activities have been scheduled and completed, and (b) it has not yet been scheduled.
Step 14: From the eligible set, identify the set of feasible activities. An activity is feasible if, in addition to being eligible, the remaining resource availability at the current time t is sufficient to accommodate its resource requirements for all resource types k: available(k, t) >= r(i, k) for all k.
Step 15: Among the feasible activities, select the activity with the highest priority according to the GRPW ranking (i.e., the activity with the lowest rank number, meaning the highest Positional Weight). If multiple activities share the same rank, apply the tie-breaking rule defined in Step 11.
Step 16: Schedule the selected activity to start at the current time t. Set its start time ST(i) = t and its finish time FT(i) = t + d(i).
Step 17: Update the resource availability profile by reducing the available units of each resource type k by r(i, k) for each time period from ST(i) to FT(i) - 1 (i.e., during the execution of activity i).
Step 18: Mark the scheduled activity as 'in progress' and remove it from the eligible set. Update the list of completed activities as their finish times are reached.
Step 19: If there are still eligible activities but none of them are feasible at the current time t (due to resource constraints), advance the current time t to the earliest finish time among all currently in-progress activities. This releases resources and may make additional activities feasible.
Step 20: Repeat Steps 13 through 19 until all activities (excluding dummy start and end) have been scheduled.
Step 21: Once all activities are scheduled, compute the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy end activity, which equals the maximum finish time among all real activities: Cmax = max(FT(i)) for all i.
Step 22: Record the complete schedule, including the start time and finish time of each activity, and verify that all precedence constraints are satisfied (i.e., ST(j) >= FT(i) for all precedence pairs i -> j).
Step 23: Verify resource feasibility by checking that at no time period t does the total resource consumption of all activities in progress exceed the available capacity for any resource type k: sum of r(i, k) for all active activities i at time t <= R(k).
Step 24: Output the final schedule with the project makespan, individual activity start and finish times, and the resource utilization profile over time as the solution to the RCPSP instance.
Least Rank Positional Weight Priority Ru:
Step 1: Parse the project network to identify all activities, their durations, resource requirements, precedence relationships, and the available resource capacities for each resource type.
Step 2: Construct the Activity-on-Node (AoN) network graph representing all activities as nodes and precedence constraints as directed arcs from predecessor to successor activities.
Step 3: Compute the Positional Weight (PW) for each activity i by summing the durations of activity i itself and all activities that follow it in all possible paths through the network (i.e., PW(i) = duration(i) + sum of durations of all successors reachable from i, considering the longest path contribution or total path sum depending on the variant used).
Step 4: Assign a Rank to each activity based on its Positional Weight. Sort all activities in descending order of their Positional Weight values so that the activity with the highest PW receives Rank 1, the next highest receives Rank 2, and so on.
Step 5: In the case of ties in Positional Weight values, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule (e.g., smallest activity index, longest duration, or most successors) to assign unique ranks to all tied activities.
Step 6: Initialize the schedule by setting the current time t = 0, marking all activities as unscheduled, and setting the available resource levels equal to the full resource capacities for each resource type.
Step 7: At the current time t, identify the set of eligible activities — those whose all immediate predecessors have been completed (i.e., their finish times are less than or equal to t) and which have not yet been scheduled.
Step 8: From the eligible activities identified in Step 7, form the Decision Set (DS) consisting of all activities that can potentially be scheduled at time t.
Step 9: Apply the Least Rank Positional Weight (LRPW) priority rule to the Decision Set: select the activity with the smallest rank number (i.e., highest positional weight) among all eligible activities as the highest priority activity to be scheduled next.
Step 10: Check resource feasibility for the highest priority eligible activity by verifying that the remaining available resource units for each resource type at time t are sufficient to meet the resource requirements of that activity throughout its duration.
Step 11: If the highest priority eligible activity is resource-feasible at time t, schedule it to start at time t, update the available resource levels by subtracting the activity's resource requirements for the duration of the activity, and mark the activity as scheduled with its start time and finish time (finish time = start time + duration).
Step 12: If the highest priority eligible activity is not resource-feasible at time t (due to insufficient resources), do not schedule it at this time. Move to the next activity in the priority-ranked Decision Set and check its resource feasibility, continuing until either an activity is scheduled or all eligible activities have been checked.
Step 13: If no eligible activity can be scheduled at the current time t due to resource constraints, advance the current time t to the next event time — defined as the earliest finish time among all currently in-progress (scheduled but not yet completed) activities — and release the resources consumed by any activities finishing at that time.
Step 14: After advancing time and releasing resources, update the set of completed activities and recalculate the eligible activity set by checking which previously ineligible activities now have all predecessors completed.
Step 15: Repeat Steps 7 through 14 iteratively, scheduling activities one at a time according to the LRPW priority rule and advancing time as needed, until all activities have been scheduled.
Step 16: Once all activities are scheduled, compute the project makespan as the maximum finish time among all scheduled activities (i.e., the finish time of the last activity to complete).
Step 17: Record the complete schedule including the start time, finish time, and resource usage for each activity, and verify that all precedence constraints are satisfied and no resource capacity is violated at any point in time.
Step 18: Optionally, evaluate the quality of the LRPW-generated schedule by comparing its makespan against known lower bounds, optimal solutions, or schedules generated by other priority rules, and use the result as an initial solution for further improvement heuristics or metaheuristic methods if needed.
Maximum Resource Utilization Priority Ru:
Step 1: Parse the RCPSP instance data by reading all project information including the set of activities J = {0, 1, 2, ..., n+1} where 0 and n+1 are dummy start and end activities, the set of renewable resources K = {1, 2, ..., m} each with capacity R_k, the duration d_j for each activity j, the resource requirements r_jk for each activity j and resource k, and the precedence relations defining immediate predecessors for each activity.
Step 2: Build the precedence network by constructing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent activities and directed edges represent finish-to-start precedence constraints, ensuring that all transitive precedence relationships are correctly captured.
Step 3: Compute the Earliest Start Time (EST) for each activity using a forward pass through the network. Set EST(0) = 0 for the dummy start activity, then for each activity j compute EST(j) = max over all predecessors i of (EST(i) + d_i), processing activities in topological order.
Step 4: Compute the Earliest Finish Time (EFT) for each activity as EFT(j) = EST(j) + d_j after completing the forward pass.
Step 5: Compute the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity using a backward pass through the network. Set LFT(n+1) = EFT(n+1) as the project deadline, then for each activity j compute LFT(j) = min over all successors i of (LFT(i) - d_i), processing activities in reverse topological order.
Step 6: Compute the Latest Start Time (LST) for each activity as LST(j) = LFT(j) - d_j after completing the backward pass.
Step 7: Compute the Total Float (TF) for each activity as TF(j) = LST(j) - EST(j) = LFT(j) - EFT(j), which represents the maximum amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion.
Step 8: Initialize the scheduling process by setting the current time t = 0, creating an empty scheduled set S = {}, adding the dummy start activity 0 to the completed set, and initializing the remaining resource availability RA_k(t) = R_k for all resources k at all time periods.
Step 9: At each scheduling decision point t, identify the set of eligible activities EL(t) defined as activities not yet scheduled whose all predecessors have been completed by time t, formally EL(t) = {j | j not in S, all predecessors i of j satisfy EFT(i) <= t}.
Step 10: From the eligible set EL(t), identify the set of schedulable activities SC(t) by checking resource feasibility, where an activity j is schedulable if r_jk <= RA_k(t) for all resources k, meaning sufficient resources are currently available to start activity j at time t.
Step 11: Compute the Maximum Resource Utilization (MRU) priority value for each schedulable activity j in SC(t). Calculate the resource utilization ratio as MRU(j) = sum over all resources k of (r_jk / R_k), which measures the total fractional resource consumption of activity j relative to the total available capacity of each resource.
Step 12: Rank all schedulable activities in SC(t) in descending order of their MRU priority values, so that the activity consuming the greatest proportion of total resource capacity is given the highest scheduling priority.
Step 13: Apply a tie-breaking rule for activities with equal MRU values. Common secondary criteria include: smallest Total Float (most critical activities first), smallest Latest Start Time, smallest activity index, or random selection among tied activities.
Step 14: Select the highest-priority activity j* from the ranked list in SC(t) as the activity to be scheduled next.
Step 15: Schedule activity j* at time t by setting its start time ST(j*) = t and finish time FT(j*) = t + d_j*, then add j* to the scheduled set S.
Step 16: Update the resource availability profile by reducing the available units of each resource k during the execution interval [t, t + d_j*) by the amount r_j*k, so that RA_k(tau) = RA_k(tau) - r_j*k for all tau in [t, t + d_j*).
Step 17: Check if there are remaining schedulable activities in SC(t) after scheduling j*. If yes, recompute SC(t) with updated resource availability and repeat the priority evaluation and selection steps to schedule additional activities at the same time point t if resources permit.
Step 18: If no more activities can be scheduled at time t (SC(t) is empty but EL(t) is non-empty or there are unscheduled activities), advance the current time t to the next event point, which is the minimum finish time among all currently executing activities: t = min over all active activities a of FT(a).
Step 19: At the new time point t, release the resources consumed by all activities that have finished at or before t by restoring RA_k(t) for those completed activities, and add those activities to the completed set.
Step 20: Repeat Steps 9 through 19 iteratively until all activities including the dummy end activity n+1 have been scheduled and completed.
Step 21: Extract the final schedule by recording the start time ST(j) and finish time FT(j) for every activity j, and compute the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy end activity: Cmax = FT(n+1).
Step 22: Validate the generated schedule by verifying all precedence constraints are satisfied (ST(j) >= FT(i) for all precedence pairs i->j), all resource constraints are satisfied at every time period (sum of r_jk for all active activities j at time t does not exceed R_k for all k and t), and all activity durations are correctly reflected.
Step 23: Optionally apply local search or improvement heuristics such as forward-backward scheduling passes, activity swapping, or integration with metaheuristics (e.g., genetic algorithms, simulated annealing) to further improve the makespan obtained by the MRU priority rule.
Step 24: Document and output the complete schedule including activity start times, finish times, resource usage profiles over time, and the final project makespan for evaluation and comparison against other priority rules or exact methods.
Critical Path Method Based Priority Rule:
Step 1: Define the project network by identifying all activities/jobs, their durations, precedence relationships (finish-to-start dependencies), and resource requirements for each resource type.
Step 2: Construct the Activity-on-Node (AoN) network diagram where each node represents an activity and directed arcs represent precedence constraints between activities.
Step 3: Perform the Forward Pass to calculate Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF) times for each activity. Set ES of the project start node to 0. For each activity, ES = max(EF of all immediate predecessors), and EF = ES + duration.
Step 4: Perform the Backward Pass to calculate Late Start (LS) and Late Finish (LF) times for each activity. Set LF of the project end node equal to the project duration (EF of end node). For each activity, LF = min(LS of all immediate successors), and LS = LF - duration.
Step 5: Calculate the Total Float (TF) for each activity using the formula: TF = LS - ES = LF - EF. Total Float represents the maximum amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the overall project completion.
Step 6: Identify the Critical Path by selecting all activities with Total Float equal to zero. These activities form the critical path, and any delay in them will directly delay the project.
Step 7: Calculate Free Float (FF) for each activity if needed: FF = ES(successor) - EF(current activity). This represents how much an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any successor.
Step 8: Assign CPM-based priority values to each activity. Activities on the critical path (TF = 0) receive the highest priority. Among non-critical activities, those with smaller Total Float values receive higher priority, as they have less scheduling flexibility.
Step 9: Initialize the scheduling process by setting the current time t = 0, marking all activities as unscheduled, and setting available resource levels to their maximum capacities for each resource type.
Step 10: At each scheduling decision point (time t), identify the set of eligible activities — those whose all predecessors have been completed by time t.
Step 11: From the eligible activities, form the Active Candidate List (ACL) by selecting activities that are ready to be scheduled (all precedence constraints satisfied and not yet started).
Step 12: Sort the Active Candidate List in ascending order of Total Float (CPM priority rule), so that activities with the least float (most critical) are scheduled first. Break ties using secondary criteria such as shortest processing time or highest resource demand.
Step 13: Iterate through the sorted Active Candidate List and attempt to schedule each activity. Check if the required resources for the activity are available at time t without exceeding the resource capacities for any resource type.
Step 14: If sufficient resources are available for an activity, schedule it to start at time t. Update the resource availability by subtracting the resource requirements of the scheduled activity from the available resource pool for the duration of the activity.
Step 15: If resources are insufficient for an activity, skip it (do not schedule it at time t) and move to the next activity in the sorted list. The skipped activity remains in the candidate list for future scheduling decision points.
Step 16: After processing all activities in the Active Candidate List at time t, advance the current time t to the next event point. The next event point is the earliest completion time among all currently executing activities.
Step 17: When activities complete at the new time t, release their consumed resources back to the available resource pool, increasing resource availability accordingly.
Step 18: Repeat Steps 10 through 17 — identifying eligible activities, sorting by CPM priority (Total Float), checking resource feasibility, scheduling feasible activities, and advancing time — until all activities have been scheduled and completed.
Step 19: Once all activities are scheduled, calculate the project makespan as the completion time of the last finishing activity. Record the schedule including start times, finish times, and resource usage for each activity.
Step 20: Evaluate the resulting schedule by comparing the makespan against the critical path length (lower bound). Analyze resource utilization profiles to assess efficiency. If needed, apply local search or improvement heuristics on top of the CPM-based schedule to further reduce makespan.
Activity Float Based Priority Rule (AFB):
Step 1: Parse the RCPSP instance data, including the number of activities (including dummy start and end nodes), the number of resource types, resource capacities for each resource type, activity durations, resource requirements per activity per resource type, and precedence constraints (finish-to-start dependencies).
Step 2: Build the precedence network by constructing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent activities and directed edges represent precedence relationships. Add a dummy source node (activity 0) with no predecessors and a dummy sink node (activity n+1) with no successors to represent the project start and end.
Step 3: Compute the Early Start (ES) time for each activity using a forward pass through the precedence network. For the source node, ES = 0. For each subsequent activity, ES = max(EF of all immediate predecessors), where EF (Early Finish) = ES + duration.
Step 4: Compute the Early Finish (EF) time for each activity as EF = ES + duration, where duration is the processing time of the activity.
Step 5: Compute the Late Finish (LF) time for each activity using a backward pass through the precedence network. For the sink node, LF = EF of the sink (i.e., the critical path length). For each preceding activity, LF = min(LS of all immediate successors), where LS (Late Start) = LF - duration.
Step 6: Compute the Late Start (LS) time for each activity as LS = LF - duration.
Step 7: Compute the Total Float (TF) for each activity as TF = LS - ES (equivalently, TF = LF - EF). Total float represents the maximum amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion.
Step 8: Compute the Free Float (FF) for each activity as FF = min(ES of all immediate successors) - EF of the current activity. Free float represents the amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any successor.
Step 9: Compute the Activity Float Based (AFB) priority value for each activity. The AFB priority is typically defined as the ratio or combination of floats, commonly expressed as AFB = TF + FF or as a weighted combination such as AFB = w1 * TF + w2 * FF, where w1 and w2 are weighting factors (often both set to 1). Activities with smaller AFB values receive higher scheduling priority.
Step 10: Initialize the scheduling process by setting the current time t = 0, marking all activities as unscheduled, and initializing the resource availability profile to the full resource capacities for all time periods.
Step 11: Identify the set of eligible activities (decision set) at the current time t. An activity is eligible if: (a) all its predecessors have been completed (i.e., their finish times are <= t), and (b) it has not yet been scheduled.
Step 12: From the eligible activities, identify the set of schedulable activities by checking resource feasibility. An activity is schedulable at time t if, for every resource type k, the remaining resource availability at time t is greater than or equal to the resource requirement of the activity for resource type k.
Step 13: Sort the schedulable activities in ascending order of their AFB priority values (lower AFB = higher priority). In case of ties in AFB values, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule such as smallest activity index, smallest duration, or largest number of successors.
Step 14: Select the highest-priority activity from the sorted schedulable list (the activity with the smallest AFB value).
Step 15: Schedule the selected activity by assigning it a start time equal to the current time t and a finish time equal to t + duration. Update the resource availability profile by reducing the available units of each required resource type for all time periods from t to t + duration - 1.
Step 16: Mark the scheduled activity as completed and remove it from the eligible and schedulable sets.
Step 17: Repeat Steps 12 to 16 (continue scheduling activities from the current schedulable set) until no more activities can be scheduled at the current time t without violating resource constraints.
Step 18: If there are still unscheduled activities but none can be scheduled at the current time t due to resource constraints, advance the current time t to the next relevant event time. The next event time is the minimum finish time among all currently active (in-progress) activities.
Step 19: Update the list of completed activities based on the new current time t. An activity is considered completed if its assigned finish time <= t. Update the resource availability profile accordingly by releasing resources freed by completed activities.
Step 20: Repeat Steps 11 to 19 until all activities (excluding dummy nodes) have been scheduled.
Step 21: Compute the project makespan as the finish time of the dummy sink node (activity n+1), which equals the maximum finish time among all real activities.
Step 22: Output the final schedule, including the start time and finish time for each activity, the total project makespan, and the resource utilization profile over time.
Step 23: Optionally, validate the solution by verifying that all precedence constraints are satisfied (i.e., for every precedence relation i -> j, the finish time of i <= start time of j) and that resource constraints are not violated at any time period.
Step 24: Optionally, apply local search or improvement heuristics (such as forward-backward scheduling passes or swapping activity sequences) to the AFB-generated schedule to further reduce the makespan if a better solution is desired.
